prompt:
Propose attack candidates for the following argument pairs from a debate on "Autonomous Vehicles".

An attack exists when one argument:
- asserts a conclusion logically incompatible with the other's conclusion — the one claim can be true only if the other is false (rebuttal), OR
- undermines a premise the other relies on (undermining), OR
- shows the other's conclusion does not follow even if its premise is true (undercutting)

Pair pro1:pro2
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro2] (pro) Self-driving cars increase road capacity by optimizing vehicle spacing.

Pair pro1:pro3
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro3] (pro) Autonomous vehicles provide mobility for people who cannot drive themselves.

Pair pro1:pro4
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro4] (pro) Self-driving technology lowers fuel consumption through more efficient driving patterns.

Pair pro1:pro5
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro5] (pro) Autonomous vehicles enable productive use of travel time for passengers.

Pair pro1:pro6
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.

Pair pro1:pro7
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro7] (pro) Autonomous fleets lower transportation costs through shared usage models.

Pair pro1:pro8
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro8] (pro) Self-driving systems can adapt routes in real time to avoid congestion.

Pair pro1:pro9
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro9] (pro) Autonomous vehicles collect data that improve urban planning decisions.

Pair pro1:pro10
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[pro10] (pro) Driverless technology accelerates innovation in sensor and artificial‑intelligence fields.

Pair pro1:con1
  A=[pro1] (pro) Autonomous vehicles reduce traffic accidents caused by human error.
  B=[con1] (con) Autonomous vehicles raise significant cybersecurity risks.

direction    = exactly one of: A_ATTACKS_B / B_ATTACKS_A / NONE
attack_type  = one of: rebuttal / undercutting / undermining / NONE
  rebuttal:     the two conclusions are logically incompatible — they negate the SAME
                proposition, so one claim is true only if the other is false. Merely
                taking the opposing stance, addressing a different sub-topic, or
                "arguing for the other side" is NOT a rebuttal; there must be a direct
                contradiction between the two specific claims. A descriptive/empirical
                claim (what people believe or do, statistics, trends) and a normative
                claim (what ought to be) are NOT logically incompatible — they cannot
                rebut each other (at most undermining/undercutting). A rebuttal is
                inherently MUTUAL (it holds in both directions); pick either direction.
  undercutting: attacker shows target's conclusion doesn't follow even if target's premise holds
  undermining:  attacker directly attacks a premise the target relies on
  If direction=NONE, attack_type MUST be NONE.
confidence   = integer 0-100: probability that ANY attack exists between the two arguments
  (0 = definitely no attack, 100 = definitely attack)
  If direction=NONE, confidence MUST be 0.

Respond ONLY with a JSON array, same order as the pairs above, no markdown:
[
  {"pair": "id_a:id_b", "direction": "A_ATTACKS_B", "attack_type": "rebuttal", "confidence": 85, "reason": "one sentence"},
  ...
]

An attack exists when one argument:
- asserts a conclusion logically incompatible with the other's conclusion — the one claim can be true only if the other is false (rebuttal), OR
- undermines a premise the other relies on (undermining), OR
- shows the other's conclusion does not follow even if its premise is true (undercutting)

Pair pro6:con2
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con2] (con) Self-driving cars can lead to massive job losses for professional drivers.

Pair pro6:con3
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con3] (con) Autonomous systems may struggle with unpredictable human behavior on the road.

Pair pro6:con4
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con4] (con) The high cost of sensor suites makes driverless cars unaffordable for many users.

Pair pro6:con5
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con5] (con) Liability determination becomes complex after accidents involving autonomous vehicles.

Pair pro6:con6
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con6] (con) Autonomous vehicles may increase urban sprawl by making long commutes easier.

Pair pro6:con7
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con7] (con) Overreliance on AI could erode human driving skills over time.

Pair pro6:con8
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con8] (con) Data collection by autonomous cars raises privacy concerns for occupants and pedestrians.

Pair pro6:con9
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con9] (con) Current regulations are insufficient to ensure the safe deployment of driverless cars.

Pair pro6:con10
  A=[pro6] (pro) Driverless cars can reduce emissions when combined with electric powertrains.
  B=[con10] (con) Autonomous vehicles can cause traffic bottlenecks when mixed with human‑driven traffic.

Pair pro7:pro8
  A=[pro7] (pro) Autonomous fleets lower transportation costs through shared usage models.
  B=[pro8] (pro) Self-driving systems can adapt routes in real time to avoid congestion.

direction    = exactly one of: A_ATTACKS_B / B_ATTACKS_A / NONE
attack_type  = one of: rebuttal / undercutting / undermining / NONE
  rebuttal:     the two conclusions are logically incompatible — they negate the SAME
                proposition, so one claim is true only if the other is false. Merely
                taking the opposing stance, addressing a different sub-topic, or
                "arguing for the other side" is NOT a rebuttal; there must be a direct
                contradiction between the two specific claims. A descriptive/empirical
                claim (what people believe or do, statistics, trends) and a normative
                claim (what ought to be) are NOT logically incompatible — they cannot
                rebut each other (at most undermining/undercutting). A rebuttal is
                inherently MUTUAL (it holds in both directions); pick either direction.
  undercutting: attacker shows target's conclusion doesn't follow even if target's premise holds
  undermining:  attacker directly attacks a premise the target relies on
  If direction=NONE, attack_type MUST be NONE.
confidence   = integer 0-100: probability that ANY attack exists between the two arguments
  (0 = definitely no attack, 100 = definitely attack)
  If direction=NONE, confidence MUST be 0.

Respond ONLY with a JSON array, same order as the pairs above, no markdown:
[
  {"pair": "id_a:id_b", "direction": "A_ATTACKS_B", "attack_type": "rebuttal", "confidence": 85, "reason": "one sentence"},
  ...
]

  {"pair":"pro6:con3","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","attack_type":"undermining","confidence":70,"reason":"Questioning autonomous systems' ability to handle human behavior undermines the premise that they will be effective enough to cut emissions."},
  {"pair":"pro6:con4","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","attack_type":"undermining","confidence":75,"reason":"High sensor costs challenge the premise that driverless cars will be widely adopted to achieve emission reductions."},
  {"pair":"pro6:con5","direction":"NONE","attack_type":"NONE","confidence":15,"reason":"Liability complexity is unrelated to the emissions‑reduction argument."},
  {"pair":"pro6:con6","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","attack_type":"undermining","confidence":65,"reason":"Potential increase in urban sprawl could offset the expected emissions benefits."},
  {"pair":"pro6:con7","direction":"NONE","attack_type":"NONE","confidence":20,"reason":"Erosion of human driving skills does not directly challenge the emissions claim."},
  {"pair":"pro6:con8","direction":"NONE","attack_type":"NONE","confidence":20,"reason":"Privacy concerns are a separate issue from emissions reductions."},
  {"pair":"pro6:con9","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","attack_type":"undermining","confidence":70,"reason":"Regulatory insufficiency undermines the premise that driverless cars will be deployed at scale to cut emissions."},
  {"pair":"pro6:con10","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","attack_type":"undermining","confidence":70,"reason":"Possible traffic bottlenecks with mixed traffic could negate the emissions‑reduction benefit."},
  {"pair":"pro7:pro8","direction":"NONE","attack_type":"NONE","confidence":10,"reason":"Both statements are complementary positive claims and do not contradict or undermine each other."}
]

Questioning autonomous systems' ability to handle human behavior undermines the premise that they will be effective enough to cut emissions.
High sensor costs challenge the premise that driverless cars will be widely adopted to achieve emission reductions.
Liability complexity is unrelated to the emissions‑reduction argument.

Erosion of human driving skills does not directly challenge the emissions claim.
Privacy concerns are a separate issue from emissions reductions.
Regulatory insufficiency undermines the premise that driverless cars will be deployed at scale to cut emissions.
Possible traffic bottlenecks with mixed traffic could negate the emissions‑reduction benefit.
Both statements are complementary positive claims and do not contradict or undermine each other.
